title: Authentication Security Rules
description: Mandatory security rules for authentication systems covering password policies (Argon2id hashing, complexity, rotation), JWT token management (RS256 signing, refresh token flow, revocation), multi-factor authentication (TOTP, SMS, email OTP), session management, account recovery, and security monitoring with anomaly detection.
anti_patterns: storing passwords in plaintext or with reversible encryption
using weak hash functions (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 without salt) for passwords
including sensitive user data (email, roles) in JWT payload
reusing refresh tokens instead of single-use rotation
no revocation list for JWTs allowing compromised tokens to persist
skipping MFA for administrative or financial accounts
allowing unlimited concurrent sessions without bounds
no account lockout after repeated failed login attempts
using SMS as primary MFA (less secure than TOTP)
allowing MFA bypass for locked accounts via recovery codes
implementation_steps: Implement password storage with Argon2id: memory cost 65536 KiB, time cost 3 iterations, parallelism 4; generate unique salt of at least 16 bytes per password using cryptographically secure RNG; store hashes and salts in a dedicated table with restricted access; never include password hashes in logs, traces, or debug output

Enforce password complexity: minimum 12 characters, at least 3 of 4 categories (uppercase, lowercase, digits, special characters), must not match top 100,000 common passwords (HaveIBeenPwned corpus), must not contain username, email prefix, or 4+ consecutive characters from display name

Implement password rotation: expire every 90 days with 14-day advance notification; prevent reuse of last 12 passwords; lock account for 15 minutes after 3 failed attempts with doubling lockout duration (15 min, 30 min, 60 min); lock until email verification after 10 failed attempts in 24 hours

Configure JWT access tokens: 15-minute expiration, RS256 signing (RSA with SHA-256); include claims: sub (user ID), iat (issued at), exp (expiration), jti (unique token ID for revocation), sid (session ID); do NOT include sensitive data (email, roles) in payload
Rotate JWT signing keys every 30 days; old keys remain valid for verification for 24 additional hours after rotation for in-flight tokens

Implement refresh token flow: 7-day opaque random strings stored in database; single-use rotation (new refresh token issued on each use, old one invalidated); store hashed in database (SHA-256 of token value); if a used-again-after-invalidation refresh token is detected (potential theft), revoke entire session and all associated tokens, notify user via email, force re-authentication; scope tokens to device and IP range

Implement token revocation: maintain revocation list in Redis with TTL equal to token's remaining lifetime; add jti to revocation list on logout, password change, or security event; API gateway checks revocation list before accepting any access token (adds ~1ms latency)

Implement MFA with three methods: TOTP (RFC 6238, 30-second time steps, authenticator apps), SMS OTP (6-digit, 5-minute expiration, fallback only), Email OTP (6-digit, 10-minute expiration, account recovery and secondary factor); MFA mandatory for admin and financial accounts; up to 3 TOTP devices and 1 SMS number per user; generate 8 single-use backup recovery codes (10 characters each)

Implement session management: server-side session tracking in database; limit to 5 concurrent sessions (oldest terminated on 6th); invalidate sessions on logout, password change, MFA enrollment change, account lock, or security-triggered revocation; track metadata (device fingerprint, IP, user agent, creation timestamp, last activity); auto-terminate idle sessions after 30 minutes; maximum session duration 24 hours regardless of activity

Implement account recovery: password reset via email with single-use token (1-hour expiration); MFA recovery requires email verification + identity verification via support ticket (government ID for high-value accounts) + 48-hour cooling period before MFA removal; recovery codes cannot bypass MFA if account is locked for suspicious activity

Implement security monitoring: log all auth events (successful logins, failed attempts, MFA challenges, password changes, session creation/termination) with full context (IP, user agent, timestamp); anomaly detection rules for new country logins, rapid sequential login attempts across accounts, mismatched IP token usage, refresh token reuse; flagged events trigger security team notification and potential automatic session revocation based on risk score threshold
rules: Password hashing must use Argon2id with memory cost 65536 KiB, time cost 3 iterations, parallelism 4
Passwords must never be stored in plaintext, reversible encryption, or weak hash functions (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 without salt)
Unique salt of at least 16 bytes per password using cryptographically secure random number generator
Password hashes and salts must be in a dedicated table with restricted access; never appear in logs, traces, or debug output
Minimum password length: 12 characters with at least 3 of 4 categories (uppercase, lowercase, digits, special)
Passwords must not match top 100,000 most common passwords (HaveIBeenPwned corpus)
Passwords must not contain username, email prefix, or 4+ consecutive characters from display name
Passwords expire every 90 days; users notified 14 days before expiration
Cannot reuse any of last 12 passwords
Account locked 15 minutes after 3 failed attempts; lockout doubles on each subsequent lockout
Account locked until email verification after 10 failed attempts in 24 hours
Access tokens: 15-minute JWT expiration, RS256 signing
JWT signing keys rotated every 30 days; old keys valid for 24 additional hours
JWTs must include: sub, iat, exp, jti, sid claims; must NOT include sensitive user data
Refresh tokens are single-use: new token issued on each use, old one invalidated
Refresh tokens stored hashed (SHA-256) in database; raw token transmitted only once
Detected refresh token reuse (potential theft) triggers full session revocation, email notification, and forced re-authentication
Refresh tokens scoped to device and IP range; different IP triggers verification step
Token revocation list maintained in Redis with TTL equal to remaining token lifetime
API gateway must check revocation list before accepting any access token
MFA is mandatory for administrative accounts and accounts handling financial transactions
Backup recovery codes are single-use; users can have up to 3 TOTP devices and 1 SMS number
Maximum 5 concurrent sessions per user; oldest terminated on 6th creation
Sessions invalidated on: logout, password change, MFA enrollment change, account lock, security-triggered revocation
Session idle timeout: 30 minutes; maximum session duration: 24 hours
Password reset tokens: single-use, 1-hour expiration via email
MFA recovery requires email verification + identity verification + 48-hour cooling period
Recovery codes cannot bypass MFA for accounts locked due to suspicious activity
All authentication events must be logged with full context (IP, user agent, timestamp)
Anomaly detection must flag: new country logins, rapid cross-account attempts, IP-mismatched token usage, refresh token reuse

examples.name: Password Hashing with Argon2id
examples.description: Hash password using Argon2id (memory 65536 KiB, time 3, parallelism 4) with unique 16+ byte salt from CSPRNG; store hash and salt in restricted-access table; never log or trace the hash

examples.name: Refresh Token Theft Detection
examples.description: If a refresh token that was already invalidated is presented again (potential theft), the system revokes the entire session and all associated tokens, notifies the user via email, and forces re-authentication from scratch

examples.name: Token Revocation Flow
examples.description: User logs out → jti added to Redis revocation list with TTL matching token's remaining lifetime → API gateway checks revocation list on every request (~1ms overhead) → revoked tokens are rejected even if not expired

examples.name: Account Lockout Escalation
examples.description: 3 failed attempts → 15 min lockout; 3 more → 30 min lockout; 3 more → 60 min lockout; 10 total in 24 hours → locked until email verification and password reset

examples.name: MFA Recovery for High-Value Account
examples.description: User loses MFA access → email verification → support ticket with government ID → 48-hour cooling period → MFA removed → user must re-enroll MFA on next login
